nodes.notes: Blame culture is a symptom of a system that has not been designed to surface and own its own failures. Structure Influences Behaviour — if the system punishes honesty about failures, people will always blame externally. The Circles of Causality reframe is the prerequisite to any meaningful system improvement: until the team owns the structure, they will keep treating symptoms.

nodes.condition: A short-term fix or manual workaround has been proposed or is already in use instead of addressing the root cause
nodes.action: Map the underlying structure before accepting any symptomatic fix. The Shifting the Burden archetype describes what happens when a workaround relieves the symptom just enough to reduce the pressure to fix the root cause — the fundamental problem remains and the symptom eventually returns, often worse. Use the 5 Whys technique to get below the symptomatic fix to the structural root. Then design a fundamental solution that removes the need for the workaround. Warn the client: every layer of workaround added makes the eventual structural fix harder and more expensive.
nodes.notes: The test for a symptomatic fix is simple: 'If we removed this workaround tomorrow, would the problem return?' If yes, it is a burden-shift — not a fix. The structural solution must be identified and planned even if the workaround is kept temporarily. Do not normalise workarounds by building the next process step around them.

nodes.branch_name: Pattern — Limits to Growth (Rapid Growth Hitting a Constraint)
nodes.condition: The business is experiencing rapid or exponential growth driven by a reinforcing loop — and needs to proactively identify what will constrain it
nodes.action: Identify the impending Balancing Loop before it activates. The Limits to Growth archetype describes what happens when a reinforcing loop — a virtuous cycle driving exponential growth — eventually encounters a balancing loop (a constraint or limiting factor) that resists further growth and causes the system to plateau or crash. The constraint is almost always invisible until it is hit. Use Capacity, Capability, Opportunity to identify which of the three — capacity ceiling, capability gap, or unvalidated opportunity — will bind first. Build a Forecast to stress-test what the business looks like if growth continues at the current rate for 12 and 24 months.
nodes.notes: The most common constraints that terminate a reinforcing growth loop: people capacity (can't hire or train fast enough), cash (growth consumes working capital faster than profit replenishes it), systems (delivery quality drops as volume exceeds process capacity), and market saturation. Identify which one is closest to binding and design for it now — not after the growth stalls.

nodes.condition: A sudden surge or drop in performance has caused the team to react immediately — changing course, over-hiring, cutting costs, or reversing a recent decision before the effects of that decision have had time to show
nodes.action: Check the system for Delay Factors before any reactive change is made. A Delay is the lag between an action and its visible feedback — the system is still responding to the last decision when the next one is already being made. Oversteering happens when leaders treat this lag as evidence that the last action failed, and reverse course prematurely. The result is oscillation: the system swings between over-correction and under-correction and never stabilises. Standardise the interpretation of data — use Volatility Analysis to distinguish common-cause variation (normal range, do not act) from special-cause events (genuine anomaly, act deliberately).
nodes.notes: Cross-reference the demings_volatility coaching tree and the data_financial_systems advisory tree for the full volatility interpretation framework. The diagnostic question is: 'Is this a special-cause event that demands a response, or common-cause variation that we are mistakenly reacting to?' Acting on common-cause variation as if it were special cause is Deming's Tampering — it adds instability rather than reducing it.

nodes.branch_name: Pattern — Finding Leverage Points in Complex Systems
nodes.condition: The team is struggling to influence complex system behaviour — there are too many variables, everything seems connected to everything else, and it is unclear where to start
nodes.action: Use Causal Loop Diagrams (CLDs) to map how the key variables in the system interact and find leverage points. A leverage point is a place in the system where a small, well-targeted change produces significant and lasting improvement across the whole system. In circular systems, intervening at the wrong point produces either no effect or an equal and opposite reaction elsewhere. The CLD maps: which variables reinforce each other (Reinforcing Loops), which variables balance and correct each other (Balancing Loops), and where the Delay Factors sit. The highest-leverage interventions are almost always structural — changing a policy, a feedback loop, or an incentive — rather than operational.
nodes.notes: Leverage points in order of increasing power (from Donella Meadows): numbers (least powerful) → feedback loop strength → information flows → rules → goals → paradigms → power to change paradigms (most powerful). Most operational fixes address numbers. The most durable improvements change the information flows or the rules. Ask: 'What is the one thing — if it changed — that would make the biggest difference to how this system behaves?'

nodes.condition: A new system improvement or plan of action is being executed — and the team needs to anticipate and measure the likely resistance or negative reactions it will generate
nodes.action: Before rollout, identify the likely opposite reactions the new system will generate and build them into the KPI set from day one. Based on Newton's Third Law — every action generates an equal and opposite reaction — no system change is consequence-free. Common reactions: a new performance measurement system generates gaming behaviour; a new efficiency process generates staff resistance and workaround creation; a new sales target generates short-term deal-rushing that undermines long-term margin. Measuring the pushback is not pessimism — it is a complete picture. If the reaction metrics are not in the KPI set, the team will only see the intended results and miss the unintended consequences until they have already compounded.
nodes.notes: The practical implementation: for every intended KPI in the new system, ask 'What behaviour does measuring this incentivise — and what is the unintended consequence of that behaviour?' Add a counter-metric that captures the reaction. For example: if measuring individual output, also measure quality and team collaboration indicators — the reaction to individual output targets is almost always a decline in both.

trees.name: Risk Management
trees.description: Guides advisors through risk identification, evaluation (heatmap), treatment strategy (Accept / Avoid / Reduce / Transfer), insurance review, and action planning including residual risk monitoring.

nodes.condition: Before asking anything — determine which risk management concern is presenting
nodes.notes: Foundation: ISO 31000 defines risk as the 'effect of uncertainty on objectives' — it can be positive (upside) or negative (downside). The goal of risk management is to reduce uncertainty about future outcomes. Key distinctions: (1) Risk Source — where the risk originates (economic shifts, natural forces, management shortcomings). (2) Risk Event — the actual occurrence that stems from the source and produces a consequence (an accident, a market crash). (3) Risk Owner — a designated person with authority and accountability to manage a specific risk. (4) Residual Risk — the risk remaining AFTER controls and treatments have been applied. Risk management is not a one-time exercise; it requires continuous monitoring and review. The 5-step ISO framework: (1) Establish Context → (2) Identify Risks → (3) Evaluate Risks (Heatmap) → (4) Determine Risk Strategy → (5) Develop Action Plan.

branches.answer_pattern: no formal risk management in place — need to build from scratch
risks have been identified but need to evaluate and decide how to treat them
specific risk event or incident has occurred or is imminent — need immediate response
insurance review — checking whether coverage is adequate, missing, or correctly structured
compliance, legal, or governance requirement driving the need for a risk framework
scenario planning — testing how the business would cope if a key person is absent, impaired, or lost

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1 — Building a Risk Framework from Scratch
nodes.condition: Client has no formal risk management — needs to build from the ground up
nodes.question: Before we identify specific risks — let's define the playing field. What timeframe are we covering with this risk plan, and are we more concerned about risks inside the business or risks coming from outside it?
nodes.notes: Step 1 — Establish Context: Define the timeframe (1 year, 3 year, project-specific). Identify internal context: governance structure, organisational capabilities, culture, key dependencies. Identify external context: local and international environment, stakeholder values, legal and regulatory trends, competitive landscape. This step prevents scope creep — a risk plan that tries to cover everything covers nothing. Step 2 — Identify Risks: Use strategic plans, SWOT analysis, historical incidents, and expert input. For each risk, identify the source (where it originates) AND the potential event (what could actually happen). Brainstorm across categories: financial, operational, people, market, legal/compliance, reputational, technology, natural/environmental. Step 3 — Evaluate: Consequence × Probability → Heatmap. Step 4 — Treat: Accept / Avoid / Reduce / Transfer. Step 5 — Action Plan with Risk Owners, timelines, contingency plans, and residual risk monitoring. The Formal Risk Management template runs this full process.

branches.answer_pattern: context established — ready to identify and map risks

nodes.condition: Context is set — now identifying and evaluating risks
nodes.question: Let's build the risk list — for each risk area, what is the risk source, what event could occur, and what is the worst realistic consequence if it did?
nodes.notes: Risk identification brainstorm categories: Financial (cash flow failure, bad debt, cost blowout, currency/interest rate); Operational (process failure, equipment failure, supply chain, quality defect); People (key person dependency, recruitment failure, culture breakdown, fraud/misconduct); Market (demand collapse, competitor disruption, pricing pressure); Legal/Compliance (regulatory breach, contract dispute, IP exposure, employment liability); Reputational (public incident, social media, client complaint escalation); Technology (data breach, system failure, cybersecurity, software dependency); Natural/Environmental (disaster, pandemic, climate-related). For each risk: name the SOURCE (economic, human, natural, technical), describe the EVENT, estimate the CONSEQUENCE (financial loss, business interruption, reputational damage) and PROBABILITY. Then plot on the Heatmap: X-axis = Probability (Low to High); Y-axis = Consequence (Low to High). The heatmap creates four quadrants that determine treatment strategy.

branches.answer_pattern: risks identified and mapped — ready to determine treatment strategy

nodes.branch_name: Branch 2 — Evaluate and Treat Identified Risks
nodes.condition: Risks are identified — need to evaluate consequence and probability and select treatment strategy
nodes.question: For each risk on the list — where does it sit on the heatmap? Is it high or low probability, and high or low consequence if it occurs?
nodes.notes: The Risk Heatmap is the central evaluation tool. Plot each risk on a matrix: X-axis = Probability (likelihood of occurrence: Rare / Unlikely / Possible / Likely / Almost Certain); Y-axis = Consequence (severity of impact: Insignificant / Minor / Moderate / Major / Catastrophic). This produces four strategic quadrants: (1) High Probability + High Consequence — priority action required: Avoid or Transfer; (2) High Probability + Low Consequence — manageable: Reduce exposure and volume; (3) Low Probability + High Consequence — insurance territory: Transfer to a third party; (4) Low Probability + Low Consequence — acceptable: Accept and monitor. Compare each mapped risk against the organisation's risk criteria (their stated risk appetite/tolerance) to determine if the current level is tolerable or requires action. Every risk that falls above tolerance threshold requires a treatment strategy and a Risk Owner.

branches.answer_pattern: high probability AND high consequence — business-threatening risk requiring urgent action
high probability but low consequence — frequent but manageable exposure
low probability but high consequence — rare but potentially catastrophic
low probability AND low consequence — within acceptable tolerance

nodes.condition: Risk sits in the top-right quadrant — likely to occur AND catastrophic if it does
nodes.question: For a risk this serious — can the activity that generates it be stopped entirely, or is it a core part of how the business operates?
nodes.notes: Treatment decision: (1) Avoid — cease the engagement or activity entirely. This eliminates the risk at its source. Best choice when the activity is not core and the risk-to-reward ratio is fundamentally unacceptable. (2) Transfer — if the activity cannot be avoided, shift the financial consequence to a third party. Insurance is the primary transfer mechanism. Other transfer tools: contractual limitation of liability, subcontracting risk to a specialist, cooperative risk pooling. Evaluate each strategy on: cost of the treatment vs certainty of the outcome. A high-cost treatment for a low-certainty outcome is rarely worthwhile. After treatment, measure the Residual Risk — what remains. If residual risk is still above tolerance, the treatment is insufficient and must be escalated. Assign a Risk Owner with formal authority and accountability for this specific risk.

branches.answer_pattern: activity can be stopped — pursuing Avoid strategy
activity is core — cannot be avoided; pursuing Transfer strategy

nodes.condition: Risk occurs frequently but individual impact is low — manageable through reduction
nodes.notes: Treatment: Reduce — limit the exposure and volume of the activity to minimise the likelihood and potential loss. Reduction tactics: (1) Process controls — documented procedures that catch errors before they escalate; (2) Frequency reduction — how often does the business engage in the activity? Can it be batched, scheduled, or rate-limited? (3) Exposure caps — set a ceiling on the maximum value or volume at risk at any one point; (4) Early warning systems — KPIs and dashboards that flag the risk materialising before it compounds. A high-frequency/low-consequence risk that is not controlled can become a high-frequency/medium-consequence risk over time through accumulation or complacency. Monitor residual risk after reduction controls are in place. Assign a Risk Owner. Build a review date into the action plan.

nodes.condition: Risk is unlikely but would be catastrophic if it occurred — primary insurance/transfer territory
nodes.notes: Treatment: Transfer — share or completely shift the risk by purchasing insurance, using a cooperative, or securing a guarantee of limited liability. Insurance is the primary tool for this quadrant: paying a premium separates the risk from the business and transfers the financial consequence to a third-party pool (a principle dating to 14th-century Genoa). Key insurance categories to review: Public/Product Liability (third-party injury or loss); Professional Indemnity (advice-based errors/omissions); Business Interruption (revenue loss from operational shutdown); Cyber/Data Breach; Key Person (loss of revenue-critical individual); Property and Equipment; Directors and Officers (D&O). The logic: even a 1% probability of a $1M loss justifies a premium if the business cannot absorb that loss. Transfer does not eliminate the risk — the Residual Risk shifts to the insurer; the business retains the deductible and any exclusions. Establish contingency and disaster recovery plans as a secondary control alongside transfer. Review coverage annually as the business risk profile changes.

nodes.condition: Risk is within acceptable tolerance — Accept and monitor
nodes.notes: Treatment: Accept — undertake the risk as currently assessed without active treatment. This is a deliberate, documented decision — not an oversight. Acceptance is appropriate when: the cost of treatment exceeds the expected value of the loss; the risk is within the organisation's stated risk appetite; controls already in place are adequate. Acceptance requires: (1) formal documentation that the risk was reviewed and accepted — protects against later claims of negligence; (2) a monitoring mechanism — even accepted risks must be watched; if probability or consequence changes, the acceptance decision must be revisited; (3) a review date — risk profiles change. Risk management is not a one-time exercise. Assign a Risk Owner even for accepted risks — someone must be watching.

nodes.branch_name: Branch 3 — Risk Event Has Occurred or Is Imminent
nodes.condition: A specific risk event has occurred or is immediately threatening
nodes.question: Has the event already happened, or is it a developing situation you can still get ahead of?
nodes.notes: Incident response sequence: (1) Contain — stop the bleeding. What immediate action prevents the consequence from worsening? (2) Assess — what is the actual consequence now that it has occurred? How does this compare to the anticipated consequence from the risk register? (3) Treat — apply the pre-determined treatment (if one exists) or select one now using Accept/Avoid/Reduce/Transfer logic. (4) Communicate — notify relevant stakeholders. Who needs to know: clients, staff, insurers, regulators, directors? Timing matters: late disclosure often worsens reputational and legal consequences. (5) Review — once stabilised, conduct a root cause analysis. Was this risk previously identified? If not, why not? If yes, why did the treatment fail? Update the risk register and controls. Contingency and disaster recovery plans should be triggered immediately where they exist. If no plan exists, the absence of one is now itself a risk to document and treat.

branches.answer_pattern: event has already occurred — in response and recovery mode
event is developing — can still act to prevent or reduce impact

nodes.condition: Risk event has occurred — managing consequence and recovery
nodes.notes: Immediate priorities: (1) Notify insurer immediately if an insurable event — late notification can void a claim; (2) Engage the Risk Owner for this risk category to coordinate response; (3) Document everything from this point — a contemporaneous record protects against later legal or regulatory challenge; (4) Assess residual risk — what further harm is still possible and what controls are now needed? Post-incident review: conduct a structured debrief once the crisis is stabilised. Root cause analysis (5 Whys) to find the fundamental source. Update the risk register: consequence and probability assumptions may need revision. Formal Risk Management runs the post-incident framework and rebuilds the risk register.

nodes.condition: Risk event is developing — preventive action is still possible
nodes.notes: Preventive action sequence: (1) Escalate immediately to the Risk Owner and decision-makers — this is not a time for committee; (2) Apply the treatment strategy from the risk register if one exists. If not, select from: Avoid (cease the activity now), Reduce (limit further exposure immediately), Transfer (notify insurer, engage legal). (3) Engage the contingency plan if one exists. (4) Communicate early to stakeholders — managing expectations early reduces reputational damage. (5) Document the developing situation in real time. The cost of preventive action is almost always less than the cost of recovery after the fact.

nodes.condition: Client wants to review insurance coverage or the Transfer strategy
nodes.question: Are you reviewing insurance because you think you may be underinsured, because something has changed in the business, or because you've never properly mapped your insurance needs against your actual risk exposures?
nodes.notes: Insurance is the primary Transfer mechanism — paying a premium shifts the financial consequence of a risk event to a third-party pool. The transfer does not eliminate the risk; it separates the business from the financial consequence. Residual risk remains: the deductible, policy exclusions, and any risks that are uninsurable. Insurance review checklist: (1) Risk profile change — has the business grown, changed activities, added new products/services, taken on contracts with new liability clauses, or hired key staff? All change the risk profile. (2) Coverage gaps — are all high-consequence risk categories covered? Common gaps: cyber liability, professional indemnity for new service lines, business interruption adequacy, key person. (3) Policy exclusions — what does the policy NOT cover? Advisors must understand exclusions before a claim, not after. (4) Adequacy of coverage limits — is the insured amount still sufficient given current revenue, assets, and client contract values? (5) Other Transfer tools beyond insurance: contractual limitation of liability clauses, subcontracting high-risk components to licensed specialists, cooperative risk pooling arrangements. Annual review is the minimum frequency; review at every material business change.

branches.answer_pattern: insurance gaps or coverage adequacy confirmed — ready to build action plan

nodes.branch_name: Branch 5 — Compliance or Governance-Driven Risk Requirement
nodes.condition: A regulatory, legal, or governance obligation is driving the need for a formal risk framework
nodes.notes: Compliance-driven risk management is not optional — it must be documented, evidenced, and reviewed on a schedule. The ISO 31000 framework is the international standard that satisfies most regulatory requirements. Key requirements for compliance-grade risk management: (1) Documented risk register with named Risk Owners; (2) Evidence of the Heatmap evaluation process; (3) Treatment strategies recorded for each risk above tolerance; (4) Formal Action Plan with tasks, owners, and timelines; (5) Contingency and disaster recovery plans for identified catastrophic risks; (6) Monitoring and review schedule — regulators expect ongoing evidence of oversight, not a one-time document. Internal context to document: governance structure, organisational capabilities, culture. External context: applicable legislation and regulations, industry standards, stakeholder expectations, legal trends. Formal Risk Management template builds this full documented framework. Where the governance structure itself is part of the risk — e.g., director liability, board oversight gaps — connect to the Governance logic tree.

nodes.branch_name: Action Plan — Treatment, Ownership, and Residual Risk
nodes.condition: Risk treatment strategy is selected — now build the formal action plan
nodes.notes: Step 5 — Develop the Action Plan. For every risk above tolerance, the action plan must specify: (1) Task — the specific control or treatment action to be implemented; (2) Risk Owner — the designated person with authority AND accountability to manage this risk. Not a committee; one named individual; (3) When — a specific deadline, not a vague timeframe; (4) Contingency Plan — if the primary treatment fails, what happens next? (5) Disaster Recovery Plan — for catastrophic risks, what is the step-by-step recovery sequence? After controls are implemented: measure the Residual Risk — the remaining level of risk after all treatments are applied. If residual risk is still above tolerance, the treatment is insufficient and must be escalated (add controls, increase transfer coverage, or Avoid). Build a review date into every risk — a risk register without a review date is not a risk management system, it is a historical document. Risk management is continuous: the risk profile changes as the business evolves, regulations shift, and market conditions change.

nodes.condition: Client wants to scenario-plan how the business copes under key person absence, impairment, or loss
nodes.question: Let's map out the scenarios. If [key person] was unable to work — how long could that absence last, and how severe would the operational and financial impact be on the business in their absence?
nodes.notes: Risk Scenario Planning uses a Duration × Impact matrix — distinct from the Probability × Consequence heatmap used for risk treatment decisions. Where the heatmap answers 'how likely and how bad?', the scenario matrix answers 'how long and how disruptive?' — it is specifically used to stress-test business continuity and key person dependency. Duration = how long the absence or disruption would last (days, weeks, permanent). Impact = the operational and financial consequence on the business during that absence. The four quadrants produce four named scenarios that give the conversation a concrete anchor. The scenarios are most commonly applied to: key person risk (owner, key manager, specialist); business continuity planning; insurance adequacy (key person insurance, income protection, business interruption); succession readiness. After mapping the scenario, connect to: the risk treatment framework (Formal Risk Management) for the appropriate treatment strategy; the Insurance review (rm_b4_insurance) if coverage gaps are identified; the Succession logic tree if the key person risk is the owner and a succession plan is absent.

branches.answer_pattern: low duration AND low impact — brief and manageable; business barely misses a beat
high duration but low impact — extended absence but business continues to function adequately
low duration but high impact — brief event but operationally or financially severe
high duration AND high impact — prolonged and catastrophic; business may not survive without intervention

nodes.condition: Low duration, low impact — the business can absorb this absence with minimal disruption
nodes.notes: Scenario: 3 Day Sickness. Low Duration + Low Impact. This scenario sits in the bottom-left quadrant — the business is resilient enough to absorb a short absence of the key person without material loss. However, resilience at this level must be validated, not assumed. Questions to probe: Are there documented processes and procedures so others can fill in immediately without the key person's knowledge? Are there cross-trained staff who can step into the role for 3 days without client or operational impact? Is there a clear delegation of authority so decisions can still be made? If the answer to any of these is 'no', the business is more exposed than the scenario suggests and the duration or impact assessment should be revisited. Action: document the cross-training and delegation arrangements as a baseline continuity measure. No urgent treatment required, but ensure residual risk is monitored as the business grows — what is manageable today may not be manageable at 2x scale.

nodes.condition: High duration, low impact — the business continues to function well over an extended absence
nodes.notes: Scenario: 6 Week Holiday. High Duration + Low Impact. The business can sustain an extended absence of the key person without significant deterioration. This is a positive indicator of systemisation and management depth. It means: documented processes are in place; management or senior staff can operate autonomously for extended periods; client relationships are not solely personal to the absent individual; financial authority is appropriately delegated. This scenario often surfaces the question of succession readiness — if the business runs well for 6 weeks without the owner, what would change under a permanent ownership transition? Connect to the Succession logic tree if succession is the underlying conversation. Insurance validation: confirm that existing income protection and key person insurance coverage periods and amounts are calibrated to this level of resilience — the business may need less coverage than assumed, which could free up premium spend for higher-consequence scenarios. Review and document the arrangements that make this resilience possible — they are a valuation asset.

nodes.condition: Low duration but high impact — a brief or sudden event with severe and lasting operational or financial consequence
nodes.notes: Scenario: Permanent Disability. Low Duration + High Impact. The triggering event may be brief (an accident, a sudden health event) but the consequence is permanent or long-lasting. This is the scenario where the absence is not temporary — the key person cannot return in the same capacity. Critical advisory questions: (1) Revenue dependency — what percentage of revenue is directly attributable to the key person's relationships, skills, or decisions? If >30%, this is a severe concentration risk. (2) Insurance coverage — does the business hold key person insurance (lump sum on incapacity or death)? Does the key person hold adequate income protection? Are the insured amounts calibrated to the revenue at risk, not just the salary cost? (3) Legal and financial structure — is the key person a director/shareholder? What are the buy-sell agreement provisions? Is there a funded buy-out mechanism? (4) Operational continuity — what systems, documentation, and management depth exist to replace the key person's function? Action: treat this as a High Consequence event on the risk heatmap. Route to rm_b4_insurance for coverage review and rm_b5_compliance if there are director liability or legal structure implications. Connect to Succession if the impaired individual is the owner.

nodes.condition: High duration AND high impact — total and permanent loss of a key person; business viability at risk
nodes.notes: Scenario: Death. High Duration + High Impact. This is the top-right quadrant — the most severe scenario in the matrix. The key person is gone permanently and the operational and financial impact is catastrophic. This scenario must be planned for even though it is uncomfortable to discuss — the absence of a plan in this scenario is itself the greatest risk. Immediate business consequences to assess: (1) Revenue — how much of current revenue is at immediate risk? What is the realistic timeline before those revenues are lost without intervention? (2) Debt and financial obligations — are there personal guarantees, director-backed loans, or key person-dependent financing that become immediately payable or at risk of default? (3) Legal structure — if the deceased is a shareholder, what does the shareholders' agreement require? Is there a buy-sell agreement? Is it funded? Without a funded buy-sell arrangement, the deceased's estate may inherit shares that cannot be sold — locking family members into a business they cannot run. (4) Insurance — is there adequate key person life insurance? Life insurance on a buy-sell agreement to fund the purchase of the deceased's equity? Business interruption coverage? (5) Succession plan — does a documented succession plan exist? Is there a named successor with the authority and capability to step in? Action: this scenario demands an immediate cross-referral to both rm_b4_insurance (coverage adequacy for death) and the Succession logic tree (emergency succession planning). A contingency and disaster recovery plan should be built immediately if one does not exist.

trees.name: Business Valuation
trees.description: Guides advisors through business sale assessments: seller pricing (6-step Advisor-e methodology, 3-point sensitivities), buyer evaluation (bankability, goodwill payback), and exit preparation (building value before sale).

nodes.condition: Before asking anything — determine whether this is a seller, buyer, or exit preparation conversation
nodes.notes: Core philosophy: place the seller in the 'negotiation seat' of the purchaser. A business is worth what a buyer can fund, not what a seller believes. The underlying method is Asset-Based: Tangible Assets (plant, machinery, equipment) + Intangible Assets (goodwill, patents, trademarks, brand) — but simply adding assets and applying a profit multiplier is insufficient and leads to overvaluation. The Advisor-e framework tests every valuation against 3-Point Sensitivities: (1) Serviceability — can the purchaser finance it? (Best Funding Calcs + interest rate risk); (2) Goodwill Repayment Period — how long to repay the goodwill component from super profits? (<3 years = Good, 3-5 = Normal, >5 = Be Careful); (3) Performance Variance — are projected net profits within a defined variance? Unrealized gains can only be included within strict limits and must be justified with specific actions and timelines. Critical distinction: EBPITDA (Earnings Before Proprietor's Income, Interest, Tax, Depreciation & Amortised Expenses) = used when buyer is hands-on / 'buying a job'. EBITDA = used for passive investors looking at distributable free cash flow only. Industry multiplier trap: published EBPIDT multiples often INCLUDE the value of tangible assets and stock — applying this multiple to goodwill alone double-counts assets and produces hyperinflated values.

branches.answer_pattern: client is the seller — wants to know what their business is worth or how to price it for sale
client is the buyer — evaluating a business to acquire and needs to assess if the price is fair
client wants to exit in the future — preparing the business to maximise sale value
management buyout, BIMBO, or Newco structure — management team or incoming management acquiring the business

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1 — Seller: What Is My Business Worth?
nodes.condition: Client wants to value their own business for a potential sale
nodes.question: Before we run the numbers — do you have up-to-date financials (P&L and balance sheet) and a list of your business assets ready, or do we need to start by gathering that information?
nodes.notes: Step 1 — Gather Core Financial and Operational Data: Balance sheet, profit and loss statements (ideally 3 years), and a comprehensive asset list. Also conduct a business overview: who are the competitors, which key personnel does the business depend on, what are the main threats, and what are the supplier relationships? This operational context is as important as the numbers — buyers price risk, not just profit. Step 2 — Calculate Adjusted Net Profit (Cash Surplus): Start with net profit and ADD BACK all expenses peculiar to the current vendor that a new owner would not incur. Common Add Backs: depreciation, interest, personal legal fees, owner's wages (if above market rate or not replaced), personal sponsorships, excessive vehicle costs, family payroll. The result is the true 'free cash flow' available to a buyer. Choose EBPITDA if the buyer will work in the business; EBITDA if the buyer is a passive investor.

branches.answer_pattern: financials are available — ready to calculate adjusted net profit

nodes.condition: Adjusted net profit is calculated — now assess assets and derive super profit
nodes.question: Let's separate out what we're actually selling. Walk me through the tangible assets, stock and materials, and intangible assets — and then we'll work out what the super profit is.
nodes.notes: Step 3 — Assess Assets and Super Profits: Separate the business into three categories: (1) Tangible Assets — physical items: plant, equipment, vehicles, fit-out. Value at market/replacement value, not book value; (2) Stock and Materials — valued at cost; (3) Intangible Assets — goodwill (customer relationships, brand, reputation), patents, trademarks, intellectual property, systems. Super Profit Calculation: Super Profit = Adjusted Net Profit (EBPITDA or EBITDA) MINUS the Minimum Acceptable Cash Surplus. The Minimum Acceptable Cash Surplus must cover: (a) A fair labour rate for the working owner — what would it cost to hire someone to replace them? (b) A basic return on invested funds — equivalent to current bank deposit rate on the purchase price; (c) A risk premium — compensation for the risk of owning a business vs a bank deposit; (d) Asset replacement reserves — provision for replacing depreciating assets. Super Profit is the earnings that remain after these baseline requirements are satisfied. It is the engine that powers the Goodwill Payback Period calculation.

branches.answer_pattern: assets categorised and super profit calculated — ready to apply the 3-point sensitivities

nodes.condition: Super profit is known — now test the proposed price against the three reality checks
nodes.question: We've got the super profit figure. Now let's test the asking price against the three sensitivities — payback period, serviceability, and performance variance. What is the proposed asking price, and how much of it is goodwill?
nodes.notes: Sensitivity 1 — Goodwill Payback Period: Goodwill Price ÷ Annual Super Profit = Years to repay. Benchmarks: under 3 years = GOOD; 3-5 years = NORMAL; over 5 years = BE CAREFUL. This is the clearest test of whether goodwill is priced fairly. A >5 year payback means the buyer is taking a speculative position, not a commercial one. Sensitivity 2 — Serviceability (Best Funding Calcs): Structure a hypothetical loan: what would the monthly repayments be at current interest rates on the full purchase price (or the funded portion)? Subtract: loan interest + principal repayments + tax + non-cash depreciation from the adjusted earnings. Is there still surplus cash for the owner? If not, the price is NOT BANKABLE regardless of how high the headline revenue is. Include an interest rate stress test — what happens if rates rise by 2%? Sensitivity 3 — Performance Variance: If the asking price relies on projected future profits rather than historical actuals, is the projection within the defined variance? Unrealized gains CAN be included but the seller must be able to identify: exactly what financial performance is yet to be realised; the specific timeframe to achieve it; the exact actions or personnel required. Vague growth projections that cannot be substantiated fail this sensitivity.

branches.answer_pattern: all three sensitivities pass — price is bankable and goodwill is justified
one or more sensitivities fail — price needs adjustment or additional justification

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1B(i) — Sensitivity Failure: Price Adjustment Required
nodes.condition: One or more sensitivities have failed — need to identify which and adjust
nodes.question: Which sensitivity is failing — is the goodwill payback period too long, the serviceability not working at the proposed price, or the performance variance not justifiable?
nodes.notes: Goodwill Payback >5 years: the goodwill price is too high. Options: (1) Reduce the goodwill component to bring payback under 5 years; (2) Justify the premium with specific, evidenced factors that de-risk the purchase (locked-in contracts, recurring revenue, documented systems, long-term leases); (3) Restructure the deal — vendor finance, earnout arrangements, deferred consideration tied to performance milestones. Serviceability failure: the total purchase price exceeds what the cash surplus can service at a reasonable loan structure. Options: (1) Reduce the total price; (2) Restructure the deal to reduce the amount requiring third-party finance (larger vendor finance component, equity contribution from buyer); (3) Improve the adjusted net profit by identifying further legitimate Add Backs. Performance Variance failure: projected profits are speculative and unsubstantiated. Options: (1) Remove unrealised gains from the valuation and price on actuals only; (2) Structure an earnout — a portion of the price is only paid if specified future performance milestones are achieved. Industry multiplier trap: if the seller has used a published EBPIDT multiple applied to the entire business — including assets and stock — the value is inflated because assets are being charged twice. Correct by applying the multiplier to goodwill ONLY and adding tangible assets and stock separately.

branches.answer_pattern: price adjusted or deal restructured — sensitivities now pass

nodes.condition: Financial sensitivities pass — now review stock/WIP position and qualitative sale risks
nodes.question: The numbers stack up. Now let's review the sale risk factors — stock and WIP position, goodwill transferability, lease situation, and staff retention provisions.
nodes.notes: Step 5 — Other Issues and Indicators: These qualitative and physical factors directly affect sale risk and buyer confidence, even when the financial model is sound. (0) Stock & WIP Verification: Before including stock at cost value, check whether any portion is perishable — calculate the percentage nearing best-by or expiry dates and adjust the included value accordingly. Buyers will not pay cost value for stock that will be unsaleable shortly after settlement. For WIP (work in progress) where scope variations exist: confirm that all variations have formal written acceptance from the client before including the WIP value in the settlement figure. Verbal or informal scope agreements are not transferable — if a dispute arises post-settlement, the buyer inherits the liability. (1) Goodwill Transferability: Is the business's goodwill attached to the owner personally (relationships, reputation, key client contacts) or to the business itself (brand, systems, contracts, recurring revenue)? Personal goodwill is the highest sale risk — a buyer is paying for something that walks out the door with the seller. Mitigation: vendor transition period, documented systems, staff continuity, key client introductions. (2) Staff Retention: Do employment agreements include retention clauses for key personnel? Key staff leaving post-sale can destroy goodwill value rapidly. (3) Lease Terms: Does the current premises lease offer favourable rollover terms without hidden reinstatement liabilities? A short remaining lease term or a landlord who can refuse assignment are significant sale obstacles. Reinstatement clauses (returning premises to original condition) can represent a large undisclosed liability. (4) Additional Working Capital: The buyer will need capital from day one. Typical provision: a 45-day trading reserve on top of the purchase price. This must be factored into the buyer's total funding requirement for the serviceability test.

branches.answer_pattern: perishable stock identified — calculate the proportion nearing best-by or expiry dates before including at cost value in the settlement
WIP exists with scope variations — verify all work in progress has formal written client acceptance before including in the settlement figure
qualitative and stock/WIP factors reviewed — move to intangible asset transferability check

nodes.condition: Qualitative sale risks reviewed — now check whether intangible assets will actually transfer with the business
nodes.question: Let's look at what's listed under intangible assets. Does the business revenue include any income from social media channels, affiliate arrangements, or platform-based traffic? And are there any licences, trademarks, patents or formal IP agreements in the business?
nodes.notes: Intangible assets appear in every valuation, but two categories carry hidden transfer risk that can destroy their stated value: (1) Social Media / Affiliate Revenue: Platform-based revenue (YouTube, Instagram, affiliate programmes, influencer arrangements) is often non-transferable. Most platform terms of service do not permit account transfer to a new owner — the account must be closed and re-opened by the new owner, losing followers, rankings, and the associated revenue. Action: review the terms of service for each platform channel generating revenue; confirm whether transfer of account ownership is permitted, requires reapplication, or is prohibited outright. If revenue is not transferable, it must be excluded from the adjusted net profit and removed from the goodwill calculation entirely. (2) Licences, Trademarks, Patents: These may have: remaining life shorter than the buyer's investment horizon (a patent expiring in 2 years on a 5-year goodwill payback is a material undisclosed risk); transfer restrictions or consent requirements from the issuing authority or regulator; renewal cost obligations the buyer must assume post-settlement. Action: obtain copies of all licence and IP agreements; confirm transferability and whether consent is required; confirm remaining life on each; build any renewal costs into the buyer's projected cost base. If these assets are not transferable or are near-expiry, they must be marked down or excluded from the intangible asset valuation.

branches.answer_pattern: social media or affiliate revenue confirmed non-transferable — excluded from adjusted net profit and goodwill calculation
licences, trademarks or patents have short remaining life or transfer restrictions — valuation adjusted accordingly
intangible assets reviewed — all transferable with no material restrictions or expiry risks

nodes.condition: All steps complete — establish the indicative valuation range
nodes.notes: Step 6 — Establish the Indicative Value Range: Use the adjusted earnings and an appropriate risk-adjusted multiplier to produce three valuation scenarios: Low (conservative — discounted for risk factors identified in Step 5), Average (market-rate — assumes normal risk profile), High (optimistic — assumes risk factors mitigated and performance variance justified). Final Assessment Value = Goodwill Component + Intangible Assets + Tangible Assets + Stock and Materials. The assessment value is an INDICATIVE RANGE, not a fixed price. Notional Market Value vs Fair Market Value: open markets fluctuate due to speculation, sentiment, and incomplete information. The notional market assumes rational actors. Business valuers must not apply open market benchmark multiples directly to a specific business — no two businesses are identical. The Advisor-e framework produces a notional market value: what a rational, informed buyer would pay given complete information. Run Business Sale Assessment 1 first (initial assessment and Add Backs), then Sale Assessment Model 2 (asset breakdown and super profit), then Sale Assessment Report 3 (final indicative range and sensitivities output). EBITDA template supports the adjusted net profit calculation.

nodes.branch_name: Branch 2 — Buyer: Is This Business Worth Acquiring?
nodes.condition: Client is evaluating a business to purchase — assessing whether the asking price is fair and fundable
nodes.question: To assess this acquisition properly — do you have the seller's financials, a breakdown of what the purchase price includes (goodwill, assets, stock), and an understanding of what your total funding requirement would be including working capital?
nodes.notes: The buyer's assessment uses the same 3-Point Sensitivities but from the opposite seat. The buyer's primary questions: (1) Can I fund it? — Serviceability test: structure the loan, calculate monthly repayments, subtract from adjusted earnings, confirm there is surplus cash after debt service. Include a 2% interest rate stress test. Add the 45-day working capital reserve to the total funding requirement. (2) Am I paying a fair price for goodwill? — Goodwill Payback Period: calculate the Super Profit, divide goodwill asking price by super profit. Under 3 years = Good. Over 5 years = Be Careful. (3) Are the projections believable? — Performance Variance: if the asking price relies on future profits, demand specific evidence: what exactly will generate the growth, what is the timeline, who will execute it? Buyer's due diligence priorities: Are key staff locked in? Can the landlord block or restrict the lease transfer? What personal goodwill is attached to the selling owner — and what is the vendor transition plan? Have all Add Backs been correctly identified — or are some recurring costs that a new owner will face? Has the industry multiplier been correctly applied to goodwill only, not the whole business? If assets and stock are included in the headline multiple, the goodwill is being double-charged.

branches.answer_pattern: financials reviewed and sensitivities applied — ready to assess the acquisition

nodes.condition: Buyer has applied the sensitivities — now make the recommendation
nodes.notes: Bankable and fair: Serviceability passes, goodwill payback under 5 years, performance variance justified. Proceed with full due diligence. Negotiate on identified risk factors (goodwill transferability, lease, staff retention). Structure the deal to include a vendor transition period and, where projections are relied upon, an earnout tied to actual performance milestones. Not bankable or overpriced: Serviceability fails OR goodwill payback >5 years without clear risk mitigation OR performance variance not substantiated. Options: (1) Negotiate price reduction to bring all three sensitivities within range; (2) Request a vendor-finance component to reduce third-party loan requirement; (3) Propose an earnout structure — base price on actuals, with additional consideration if projected performance is achieved within a defined period; (4) Walk away — if the seller will not adjust and the sensitivities cannot be made to work, the price is speculative, not commercial.

nodes.branch_name: Branch 3 — Exit Preparation: Building Value Before Sale
nodes.condition: Client plans to sell in the future — preparing the business to maximise sale value
nodes.question: Let's identify what is currently holding the value down. If we ran the valuation today — which of the three sensitivities do you think would cause the most concern: the goodwill payback period, the serviceability, or the performance story?
nodes.notes: Exit preparation is about systematically removing the risk factors that depress valuation and strengthen the factors that support it. The three value levers: (1) Increase Adjusted Net Profit — review Add Backs to confirm the financial picture is as clean as possible. Are there owner-specific expenses currently hidden in costs that should be legitimately added back? Is the owner's wage correctly benchmarked to market rate? Is depreciation correctly applied? A higher adjusted net profit directly increases both the valuation range AND the serviceability test. (2) Improve Goodwill Transferability — the highest value businesses are those where the goodwill is attached to the BUSINESS, not the owner. Actions: document all systems and processes (systemise before you sell); build a management team that can run the business without the owner; convert personal client relationships to business relationships through CRM, communication trails, and documented account management; build recurring revenue and contracted income. (3) Address Structural Sale Risks — negotiate a long-term lease with favourable rollover terms before going to market. Implement staff retention agreements for key personnel. Clear any reinstatement liabilities in the lease. Build a 45-day working capital buffer into the balance sheet. Timeline: exit preparation typically takes 2-3 years to meaningfully shift valuation. Advisors who engage clients on exit preparation early create significant long-term advisory value.

branches.answer_pattern: primary constraint is profitability — adjusted net profit needs to improve
primary constraint is goodwill risk — business is too dependent on the owner
primary constraint is structural — lease, staff, or working capital issues

nodes.condition: Client's valuation is limited by low adjusted net profit — needs to improve earnings
nodes.notes: Adjusted net profit is the foundation of every valuation metric. Increasing it by $50,000 can shift the goodwill value by $150,000-$250,000 on a 3-5x multiplier, while simultaneously improving the serviceability and payback period. Actions: (1) Complete a full Add Backs review — ensure every legitimate owner-specific expense is identified and documented; (2) Benchmark owner's wage to market rate — if the owner is being paid above market, the Add Back is legitimate; if below, future buyers will add the shortfall back as a cost; (3) Review product/service mix — shift revenue toward higher-margin lines to grow gross profit without growing costs; (4) Review fixed cost structure — unnecessary fixed costs directly reduce net profit with no Add Back benefit; (5) Build 2-3 years of clean, consistent profit history — buyers discount volatile or single-year profit spikes. The EBITDA template supports adjusted net profit calculation and Add Back documentation.

nodes.condition: Business value is concentrated in the owner — goodwill will not transfer without them
nodes.notes: Personal goodwill is the largest single discount factor in any business sale. A buyer paying for goodwill that leaves with the seller is taking a speculative risk, and will either refuse to pay for it or demand a long vendor earnout tied to client retention. Building transferable goodwill: (1) Systemise — document every core process. A buyer must be able to see how the business runs without relying on the owner's knowledge. Use Process Session to map and document systems; (2) Build management depth — if the owner is the only decision-maker, the business cannot run post-sale. Develop a management layer that operates independently; (3) Convert client relationships — move client relationships from owner-personal to business-systemic: CRM records, service agreements, communication trails, account management by staff not just the owner; (4) Build recurring revenue — contracted, subscription, or retainer income that survives an ownership change is worth a premium multiple; (5) Create a transition plan — buyers value a structured vendor transition period (6-12 months) that transfers knowledge and relationships systematically.

nodes.condition: Lease terms, staff agreements, or working capital are creating sale obstacles
nodes.notes: Structural sale risks are often overlooked until a buyer's solicitor finds them during due diligence — at which point they become price chips or deal-breakers. Address them before going to market. Lease: negotiate a new long-term lease with at least 3-5 years remaining plus renewal options before listing. Confirm the landlord will consent to lease assignment to a new owner. Identify and quantify any reinstatement obligations (cost to return premises to original condition) — these are real liabilities that buyers will deduct from their offer. Staff Agreements: implement written employment contracts with key staff including reasonable notice periods. Where appropriate, add retention bonuses tied to a successful sale and post-settlement employment for a defined period. Key person dependency is a valuation risk — cross-train wherever possible. Working Capital: build a 45-day trading reserve into the balance sheet before sale. Buyers need to fund working capital on top of the purchase price; if the business has no cash buffer, buyers will either reduce their offer or require vendor finance to cover the working capital requirement.

nodes.condition: The acquisition involves a management team buying the business rather than an external third-party buyer
nodes.question: Before we run the numbers — is this a Management Buyout (existing management team purchasing), a BIMBO (an incoming manager buying alongside the existing team), or a Newco structure (a new company formed to acquire the business)? And critically — who controls the negotiation on the buy side?
nodes.notes: MBO, BIMBO, and Newco are distinct transaction structures, each with different advisory implications: (1) Management Buyout (MBO): the existing management team acquires the business. Key risk: incumbent management has insider knowledge of the business's weaknesses, vulnerabilities, and key person dependencies — the seller is at an information disadvantage. Management loyalty is divided during the negotiation period. The vendor must obtain independent valuation advice and legal advice to avoid being disadvantaged by their own team. Funding: typically structured with a combination of personal equity from the management team, bank debt, and vendor finance. Earnout provisions are common — a portion of the price paid only if the business performs to forecast post-settlement. (2) BIMBO — Buy-In Management Buyout: an external incoming manager or management team partners with the existing internal team to acquire the business. Introduces an external perspective that can rebalance the insider knowledge asymmetry. The incoming manager typically brings equity capital; the internal team brings operational continuity and client relationships. Advisory focus: equity split arrangements between incoming and incumbent management, respective roles post-settlement, and alignment of incentives. (3) Newco Structure: a new company (Newco) is formed specifically to acquire the target business. Common reasons: asset protection, clean balance sheet, specific tax structuring, or investor syndicate requirements. Advisory focus: Newco shareholder identities and equity proportions, funding sources (equity vs debt), governance structure post-acquisition, and whether the acquisition triggers change-of-control clauses in key contracts, leases, or licences. For all three structures: run the same 3-Point Sensitivities (serviceability, goodwill payback, performance variance) but from the Newco or acquiring entity's combined funding position. Check all key contracts and leases for change-of-control clauses — these can create immediate renegotiation obligations or termination rights that materially affect valuation.

branches.answer_pattern: MBO — existing management purchasing; apply sensitivities from management team's combined funding position and address information asymmetry risk for the vendor
BIMBO — incoming manager plus existing team; clarify equity split and funding structure before applying sensitivities
Newco — new entity formed for acquisition; confirm shareholder structure, funding, and change-of-control clause exposure before applying sensitivities

trees.name: Succession Planning
trees.description: Guides advisors through internal succession engagements: family business handovers, management succession, and emergency sudden-departure planning. Uses the Dream Home methodology, IFAC Value Preservation lifecycle, ISO 22301 dual-track planning, and OECD governance principles.

nodes.condition: Before asking anything — determine the nature and urgency of the succession conversation
nodes.notes: Note: if the discussion is about selling to an external third party, redirect to the Valuation logic tree. Succession applies to internal handovers: family, management, or key staff. Three distinct conversation types exist and each requires a different entry path: (1) Family succession — an owner handing the business to a family member (child, spouse, sibling). Emotionally complex, legally intricate, high conflict risk. Use the Dream Home methodology. (2) Management or key staff succession — an identified internal candidate (non-family) being developed to take over a role or the whole business. Less emotionally charged but requires a rigorous development framework. (3) Emergency-only succession — no active succession plan exists, owner wants to establish a 'sudden departure' safety net for the business. ISO 22301 dual-track planning applies. Core philosophy (from the Argumentative Theory of psychology): humans under succession stress default to self-interest arguments. A structured framework is essential because it secures agreement on how the problem will be broken down before jumping to solutions, which prevents defensive conflict. The advisor's role is facilitation, not advice — let go of a predetermined outcome and chair the discussion instead.

branches.answer_pattern: family member will be the successor — this is a family business handover
management or key staff member is the identified successor — internal non-family succession
no succession plan in place — need a sudden departure or business continuity safety net only

nodes.condition: This is a family succession — assess whether the incumbent has begun thinking about life after the business
nodes.question: Before we get into the detail — has [owner] had a genuine chance to think about what life after the business looks like for them personally? Or are we still at the very beginning of that conversation?
nodes.notes: This is the most important gate in any succession engagement. The greatest single predictor of failure is an incumbent who agrees to succession intellectually but has not emotionally processed the loss of identity, purpose, and relevance that business exit brings. Indicators the incumbent is NOT ready: vague timelines ('sometime in the next few years'), reluctance to let successors make decisions, micromanaging the handover process, repeated reversion to 'I'm not ready yet'. If NOT ready: do not proceed to the wish list meetings — premature structured planning before emotional readiness solidifies conflict. Instead, use discovery questions to surface what the business gives the owner beyond income (identity, purpose, community, structure) and discuss what replaces those needs post-exit. A structured Initial Discovery Questionnaire directed at the incumbent — exploring their vision of life after business, financial requirements, and legacy goals — is the appropriate starting point. This creates a private baseline readiness report before formal meetings begin. If READY: proceed to the separate wish list meetings (Steps 2 and 3).

branches.answer_pattern: incumbent has thought about this and is emotionally ready to begin the process
incumbent has not fully processed what life after business looks like — not emotionally ready

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1A — Incumbent Not Ready: Initial Discovery
nodes.condition: Incumbent has not emotionally processed the transition — premature planning will backfire
nodes.notes: Step 1 of the Dream Home Methodology: Initial Discovery. Direct the incumbent to an introductory digital questionnaire before any formal meetings begin. Key questions to explore: What will you do with your time? What will give you purpose? What income do you need in retirement, and for how long? What does your ideal handover timeline look like? What do you want the business legacy to be? The questionnaire output becomes a private baseline readiness report — the advisor reviews it before any family meeting. This protects the process: walking into a family succession meeting with an unready incumbent typically produces an emotionally charged first session that entrenches positions rather than building alignment. The visual metaphor to use with clients: frame the succession journey as building a dream home. Just as an architect wouldn't start drawing plans before understanding the family's lifestyle and requirements, the advisor cannot design a succession plan before understanding what the incumbent is stepping into, not just stepping out of.

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1B — Wish List Meetings: Incumbents and Successors Separately
nodes.condition: Incumbent is ready — now capture wishes from both parties separately before any joint meeting
nodes.question: Have we had a chance to meet with the owners and successors separately yet, or are we jumping straight into a joint conversation?
nodes.notes: Steps 2 and 3 of the Dream Home Methodology — conduct these as SEPARATE meetings, NEVER jointly at this stage. Step 2 — The Parents' Wish List: Meet with the incumbents only. Capture: (1) Family considerations — what is fair and why? Which family members are involved and on what basis? (2) Financial requirements — what retirement income is needed, what debts are being cleared, what lifestyle is expected? (3) Successor expectations — what does the incumbent expect from the successor going forward? What is non-negotiable? Step 3 — The Successors' Wish List (Kids' Wish List): Meet with the successors only. Capture: (1) Their vision for the business — what do they want to change, grow, or protect? (2) Their financial position — what can they realistically afford? What does the handover cost them personally? (3) Their expectations of the incumbents post-succession — ongoing involvement, advisory roles, complete exit? Critical confidentiality rule: if there is family disharmony, keep both wish list documents completely separate and hidden from the other party until the Concept Meeting (Step 4). Premature disclosure of wish lists in a high-conflict family context triggers the Argumentative Theory dynamic — stakeholders entrench into their stated positions and negotiation becomes adversarial rather than collaborative.

branches.answer_pattern: both wish list meetings completed — ready to bring parties together for the concept meeting
wish list meetings not yet done — starting here
family disharmony exists between siblings — need to manage confidentiality before proceeding

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1B(i) — Sibling Dynamics: Managing Family Disharmony
nodes.condition: Active family disharmony exists between siblings or family members involved in the succession
nodes.notes: IF there is current family disharmony between siblings, THEN keep the Kids' Wish List document completely hidden from all other parties until the Concept Meeting. This is not optional — premature disclosure of the successors' wish list in a high-conflict family context will cause stakeholders to entrench into defensive positions before the collaborative framework has been established. Dan Sperber's Argumentative Theory explains why: humans under stress default to arguments that serve their own survival interests. Seeing the other party's wish list before a structured facilitation context exists activates that defensive instinct and turns a wish list comparison into a negotiation battle. Practical management: store each wish list document separately with access restricted to the advisor and the relevant party only. Do not summarise, paraphrase, or reference specific items from one list in any communication with the other party. At the Concept Meeting (Step 4), the advisor controls the reveal — placing both lists on the table simultaneously in a facilitated environment where the framework for discussion has already been agreed. If the disharmony is severe enough that a joint meeting carries significant risk of breakdown: consider a structured pre-meeting with each party separately to set explicit ground rules for the Concept Meeting before bringing them together. In extreme cases, a professional family mediator should be engaged alongside the advisor. The advisor's role remains facilitation, not adjudication.

nodes.condition: Wish lists captured — before the concept meeting, run a financial viability check
nodes.question: Before we bring the family together — do the numbers actually work? Can the successor fund the transition without it being financially crippling, and can the incumbent's retirement income requirements be met from what the business generates?
nodes.notes: Financial viability is the most common reason family successions collapse after the relationship work has been done. Core check: (1) Transfer costs — what is the total cost of the ownership transfer? Includes: stamp duties, legal costs, valuation fees, tax liabilities (capital gains, gift duties if applicable). Are these costs manageable, or do they consume so much value that the incoming successor is immediately burdened? (2) Successor funding capacity — can the successor fund their share of the acquisition? What is the net cost to them after any discounts, gifting, or vendor finance arrangements? Is the monthly repayment burden sustainable from the business's cash flow after the succession takes effect? (3) Incumbent retirement income — does the business generate sufficient income to fund the incumbent's retirement requirements (lump sum extraction, ongoing income, superannuation arrangements) while still leaving the successor with a viable business? Use the Valuation logic tree (Business Sale Assessment framework) to establish a notional fair value before the financial viability check. Key fairness question (Q6 from succession framework): were the successors previously underpaid while building the business? If so, this underpayment may justify a discount on the transfer price — the family must discuss and agree the methodology explicitly. Use the Salary Sacrifice table to calculate the real-world income loss for a sibling or successor who was chronically underpaid or who provided personal care to ageing parents while siblings pursued external careers.

branches.answer_pattern: financially viable — the transfer costs are manageable and both parties' income needs can be met
financially problematic — transfer costs are too high, funding is unworkable, or retirement income falls short
siblings are receiving disparate financial figures — equity methodology needs to be established
incoming successor was chronically underpaid while building the business — sweat equity discount may apply

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1C(ii) — Disparate Financial Value: Sibling Equity Methodology
nodes.condition: Siblings involved in the succession are slated to receive different financial outcomes — the methodology must be explicitly agreed
nodes.notes: IF siblings are receiving disparate financial figures, THEN utilise the Salary Sacrifice table to calculate the real-world loss of income for those who provided elderly or family care, and document the agreed equity methodology transparently before the Concept Meeting. Disparate outcomes in family succession are common and legitimate — but only when the reason is explicit, agreed by all parties, and anchored in objective rules. Common legitimate reasons for disparate figures: (1) One sibling worked in the business for years at below-market wages — their labour contribution has an economic value that must be quantified and credited; (2) One sibling provided primary care for ageing parents, forgoing career income — the Salary Sacrifice table calculates the real-world income loss over the care period; (3) One sibling received prior financial gifts or support from the parents — these must be counted against their succession entitlement if equity is the agreed goal; (4) Siblings have different roles in the future business — an operating successor contributing ongoing labour and risk should receive different consideration than a passive recipient. The Salary Sacrifice calculation: identify the period during which the sibling was underpaid or providing care; calculate the difference between fair market rate and what was actually received or earned; this figure represents a legitimate economic claim that should be offset against their succession payment or credited as an equity adjustment. Transparency requirement: if different figures are provided to different siblings, the methodology and reasons must be thoroughly discussed and agreed by ALL parties — not unilaterally decided by the incumbent. Establishing a Family Constitution early creates the objective framework within which these calculations are accepted as fair rather than contested as arbitrary.

nodes.condition: The incoming successor was chronically underpaid relative to market rate while building the business — a purchase price discount may be warranted
nodes.notes: IF the incoming successor was chronically underpaid while building the business, THEN negotiate whether a discount should be applied to their final purchase price to compensate for the sweat equity contributed at below-market wages. This is a distinct calculation from the Disparate Financial Value branch (which addresses sibling equity across multiple recipients). This branch applies when a single successor — typically a child who joined the business early and accepted below-market wages in exchange for a future ownership stake — is now being asked to pay a full commercial price for a business they helped build. The core question: were the wages previously paid to the successor at a fair market rate? If not, the family must quantify the cumulative shortfall. Calculation approach: (1) Identify the period during which the successor was employed at below-market wages; (2) Establish the fair market rate for an equivalent external hire in that role for each year of the period; (3) Calculate the annual shortfall (market rate minus actual wages paid); (4) Sum the total cumulative underpayment over the full period — this is the sweat equity figure; (5) The family then negotiates what portion of this figure is credited as a discount against the purchase price. There is no universal rule — some families credit 100% of the shortfall, others negotiate a partial credit. What matters is that the methodology is agreed explicitly and documented before finalising the transfer price. Connect to the Salary Sacrifice table for the calculation. Ensure the agreed discount methodology is recorded in the Family Constitution or the formal succession agreement.

nodes.condition: The succession is not financially viable as currently structured — need to identify what is blocking it
nodes.question: Is the problem primarily the transfer cost burden on the successor, the retirement income shortfall for the incumbent, or both?
nodes.notes: Transfer cost burden on successor: (1) Vendor finance — the incumbent provides a loan to the successor for part of the purchase price, repaid from business distributions over time. Reduces the successor's need for third-party debt. (2) Staged acquisition — the successor acquires an agreed percentage of equity per year, funded from their share of distributions, over a 3-5 year transition. Reduces single-point financing risk. (3) Gifting component — the incumbent gifts a portion of value to the successor (tax implications must be confirmed by legal/tax advisors). (4) Discount for past underpayment — if the successor was chronically underpaid, a formally agreed and documented discount reduces the effective transfer price. Incumbent retirement income shortfall: (1) Improve business performance before succession — a more profitable business generates more extraction capacity for the incumbent AND a higher notional value for the successor. Engage the relevant performance advisory tools first. (2) Recalibrate timeline — the succession may need to be deferred by 2-3 years while the business performance improves and the incumbent builds a separate retirement fund. (3) Restructure the income arrangement — consider an ongoing consulting fee, director's fee, or profit-share arrangement where the incumbent retains an income role post-succession without retaining operational control. If neither fix is viable with current business performance, the succession should be deferred and business improvement work prioritised first.

branches.answer_pattern: financial structure resolved — proceed to governance discussion

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1D — Governance Structure: Ownership vs Stewardship
nodes.condition: Financial viability confirmed — now establish the governance framework before the concept meeting
nodes.question: Here's a critical question for the family to answer: when the business transfers, are the new owners expected to 'own' it outright — to do with it as they choose — or are they acting as stewards, preserving it for the next generation after them?
nodes.notes: This is one of the most emotionally loaded questions in any succession engagement and it must be answered explicitly before the concept meeting, not assumed. The OECD Principles of Corporate Governance (for family enterprises) prescribe a clear Separation of Powers between three distinct roles: (1) Ownership — who holds the economic interest and voting rights? (2) Governance — who sets the strategic direction and holds the board function? Who can override decisions? (3) Management — who runs the business day-to-day? In many family successions, the incumbent attempts to retain Governance or Management influence after transferring Ownership — this is the primary source of post-succession conflict. The conflict is predictable and preventable only if the separation is made explicit and written down. Family Constitution: the OECD strongly recommends a written Family Constitution for all family enterprise successions. The Family Constitution documents: the agreed rules for family involvement in the business; how disputes between family members will be resolved; the agreed process for future succession events; the conditions under which family members can sell or gift their interest; the rules for remuneration of family members employed in the business. A Family Constitution does not replace legal documents (shareholder agreements, wills, trusts) — it establishes the agreed family rules that sit above and guide those legal structures. Sibling equity (Q5): if multiple siblings are involved and receiving different financial arrangements, the Family Constitution must document the agreed methodology and the reasons — transparency is non-negotiable. Use the Salary Sacrifice table where siblings provided unequal labour or caregiving contributions.

branches.answer_pattern: governance structure agreed and family constitution scope defined — ready for the concept meeting
significant governance conflict — incumbents want to retain control after transfer

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1D(i) — Governance Conflict: Incumbent Retaining Control
nodes.condition: Incumbent wants to transfer ownership but retain decision-making authority — classic conflict point
nodes.notes: This is the most common and most destructive pattern in family succession: the incumbent transfers the ownership but retains the governance function informally, creating an unresolvable authority conflict for the successor. Why it happens: the incumbent has not separated their identity from the business. 'Handing over' feels like losing relevance, not gaining freedom. The successor is left managing upward (waiting for permission) while nominally owning the business. Resolution framework: (1) Define a written exit timeline for the incumbent's operational authority — a structured phased withdrawal, not an open-ended one; (2) Create a formal Governance role for the incumbent that is defined and bounded — e.g., Chair of an Advisory Board with a defined scope and term; (3) Separate the governance function from management: the incumbent can retain a governance/advisory role without having operational veto power; (4) Use the visual Ascension Diagram with the family: the successor must be shown moving upward in the diagram — the incumbent's continued involvement must be framed as mentorship, not supervision; (5) If conflict cannot be resolved, the succession is not ready to proceed — return to the readiness conversation with the incumbent. Forcing a succession over a governance conflict produces litigation, not transition.

nodes.branch_name: Branch 1E — Concept Meeting: Bring Both Parties Together
nodes.condition: Financial viability and governance structure established — now bring stakeholders together to reconcile wish lists
