nodes.condition: Client ties up cash in rare items or loses money on perishable or slow-moving stock
nodes.action: Deploy direct-to-customer air freight for rare items and fast freight for perishables — fast freight maximises the 'sales days' window to recover investment before stock perishes. Negotiate delayed supplier payment terms for slow-moving lines to reduce cash commitment before a sale is confirmed.
nodes.notes: Stock management is a cash management discipline. Reducing the holding period of slow-moving or high-risk stock directly improves the working capital cycle and reduces write-off risk.

nodes.condition: Client's team loses deals when prices are challenged, leading to discounting and margin erosion
nodes.action: Conduct market research to confirm pricing sits in the lower end of the upper-most quadrant — hold the price rather than discount. Develop scripts for the top 5 sales objections and enforce buddy rehearsals so the team builds confidence in holding price under pressure. Cross-reference the sales_marketing and client_sales advisory trees for a broader sales process review if conversion is the wider issue.
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Confidence in overcoming objections allows pricing to be maintained without sacrificing conversion rates. Discounting almost always signals value uncertainty to the buyer and is rarely the right answer.

trees.name: Strategy & Planning
trees.description: Master advisory triage for strategy and planning conversations. Routes by the nature of the strategic need — business planning, vision and identity, pitch documents, pivot/direction change, or growth strategy. Cross-references client_planning, frameworks_find, profitability_feasibility, and the coaching trees (heald_matrix, capacity_capability_opportunity, reveal_growth_curve) rather than duplicating their content.
entry_triggers: what do we want to become

nodes.branch_name: Initial Split — What Is the Strategic Need?
nodes.question: Is this about building or updating a business plan, defining or realigning the vision and identity of the business, preparing a pitch or investor document, pivoting or changing strategic direction, planning for growth or scaling, or is the client struggling to engage with strategy altogether?
nodes.notes: This tree routes to existing advisory and coaching trees rather than duplicating them. The client_planning tree handles structured business planning; frameworks_find handles vague or undifferentiated presenting problems; the coaching trees (heald_matrix, capacity_capability_opportunity, reveal_growth_curve) handle facilitation delivery. This tree fills the gaps between them.

branches.answer_pattern: business plan / planning / need a plan / planning session / planning structure
vision / mission / why / ethos / purpose / values / direction / identity / what do we stand for
pitch document / pitch deck / investor / capital raise / raise money / present to investors / investor ready
pivot / change direction / new strategy / fundamental shift / business model change / change what we do
scale / scaling / grow / growth strategy / growth plan / how do we grow / next phase of growth
stuck / avoidant / too busy to plan / overwhelmed / can't focus / don't know where to start strategically

nodes.condition: Client needs a business plan or structured planning session
nodes.action: Cross-reference the client_planning advisory tree. It routes by business maturity: Startup (1pg or 4pg Bizz Case + Revenue Model), New to Planning (Lite Strategy + Revenue Model), or Established Business (Planning Outcomes Review → Business Targets / Strategic Orientation / Organisational Review / Sales & Marketing Review).
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Use the client_planning tree for full routing logic. Both the startup and established paths are supported by a Revenue Model.

nodes.branch_name: Vision, Mission & Identity — First Time or Realigning?
nodes.question: Is this about defining the vision, mission, and ethos for the first time — the business has never articulated it clearly — or is it about realigning because the identity has drifted or the team is no longer connected to it?
nodes.notes: Vision and identity work is foundational. Without a clear answer to 'what do we want to become and why', all planning, target-setting, and strategic orientation work builds on sand. The distinction between first-time definition and realignment matters because the tools are different — definition work builds from scratch, realignment work diagnoses the gap between intended and current state.

branches.answer_pattern: first time / never defined / no clear vision or mission / starting from scratch
had it before / drifted / lost / team not aligned / culture changed / not living the values

nodes.condition: Business has never clearly defined its vision, mission, values, or strategic purpose
nodes.action: Use Strategic Orientation Part 1 to establish the foundational identity: vision (what we want to become), mission (what we do and for whom), values (how we behave), and strategic positioning. Follow with Powerful Goal Setting and Business Targets to convert the identity work into measurable objectives and milestones.
nodes.notes: Strategic identity work must precede target-setting — you cannot set meaningful goals without knowing what you are trying to become. Orientation Part 1 anchors the why; Orientation Part 2 explores competitive positioning and market strategy.

nodes.branch_name: Identity — Realigning a Drifted Culture or Direction
nodes.condition: Business had a clear vision or values but the team has drifted from them, or the strategic direction has changed without the culture catching up
nodes.action: Use Alignment Statements to audit the gap between stated values and observable behaviour, and the Organisational Review to diagnose structural or cultural causes of the drift. Follow with Strategic Orientation Part 1 to formally restate and recommit to the identity.
nodes.notes: Culture drift is almost always a leadership problem — the identity was not actively reinforced. The Alignment Statements template surfaces the gap in plain language so the team can own the disconnect rather than defend against it.

nodes.question: Is this pitch document for an external audience — investors, a bank, or a capital raise — or is it an internal strategic document for the board, leadership team, or a key stakeholder?

branches.answer_pattern: external / investors / bank / capital raise / funding / raise money / present to investors / lender
internal / board / leadership team / key stakeholder / strategic summary / present to partners

nodes.branch_name: Pitch — External Investor or Capital Raise
nodes.condition: Client needs to prepare a pitch document for investors, a bank, or a capital raise
nodes.action: Build the pitch around the 1pg or 4pg Bizz Case as the narrative structure, supported by a Revenue Model to demonstrate the business logic and a Forecast with Assumptions to evidence the financial projections. The pitch must answer: what problem does it solve, for whom, how does it make money, what does it need, and what does the investor get. The Loan Estimator is relevant if the capital raise involves debt.
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Investors and lenders want to see: a clearly defined problem and solution, evidence of market demand, a credible financial model, and a realistic picture of what success looks like. Use the Revenue Model to stress-test the numbers before the pitch — if it does not hold up in the model, it will not hold up in front of an investor.

nodes.condition: Client needs to prepare an internal strategic document for the board, leadership team, or key partners
nodes.action: Use Strategic Orientation Part 1 and Part 2 to structure the strategic narrative, with Alignment Statements to ensure the document reflects shared values and direction. Powerful Goal Setting converts the strategy into a clear set of objectives and milestones the team can commit to.
nodes.notes: An internal pitch is a consensus-building tool, not a selling document. Its purpose is alignment — every reader should leave with the same understanding of where the business is going and what their role in it is.

nodes.branch_name: Pivot — External Disruption or Internal Strategic Choice?
nodes.question: Is the pivot being driven by external forces — market disruption, a technology shift, competitive pressure, or an industry change the business must respond to — or is it an internal strategic choice, where the owner wants to change direction or the current model is simply not working well enough?
nodes.notes: The distinction matters because it determines the starting tool. External disruption requires competitive repositioning frameworks. Internal-choice pivots require viability testing before committing to the new direction — the Cautious Reveal and Lite Feasibility pathways are designed exactly for this.

branches.answer_pattern: external / market disruption / technology shift / competition / industry change / forced to change / must respond
internal / owner wants to change / model not working / strategic choice / not happy with direction / want to do something different

nodes.condition: Business must pivot in response to market disruption, a technology shift, competitive pressure, or an industry change
nodes.action: Use Porter's & Pine to map the competitive landscape and identify where the business can reposition. Follow with Blue Ocean Strategy to surface untapped market space — the goal is to find a position the disruption cannot easily reach. Reveal the Growth Curve to show where the business is on its growth journey and what the pivot means for the next stage.
nodes.notes: External pivots are urgent but must still be strategic. Do not simply react to the disruption — use the competitive frameworks to find the position of strength before committing to the new direction. Speed matters, but pivoting into the wrong space is worse than pivoting slowly into the right one.

nodes.branch_name: Pivot — Internal Choice: Validated or Unproven?
nodes.question: Has the new direction been tested or validated with real market feedback — customers, pilot sales, or qualitative evidence of demand — or is it still at the idea or intention stage with no external proof yet?
nodes.notes: This is the single most important question for any internally-driven pivot. Enthusiasm for a new idea is not evidence of market demand. The Cautious Reveal and Lite Feasibility methods exist specifically to test unproven pivots before the business commits capital or operational resources to them.

branches.answer_pattern: validated / has market evidence / customers have expressed demand / tested / pilot completed / proven concept
unproven / idea stage / no evidence yet / gut feel / hasn't been tested / not sure if there's a market

nodes.branch_name: Pivot — Validated Direction: Plan the Transition
nodes.condition: New strategic direction has been tested and validated with real market evidence
nodes.action: Use the Planning Outcomes Review to structure the pivot as a formal strategic plan. Depending on what the review surfaces: Strategic Orientation (to restate vision and positioning for the new direction), Powerful Goal Setting (to set the milestones for the transition), and a Revenue Model or Forecast to map the financial trajectory of the pivot.
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: A validated pivot still needs a structured transition plan — the business does not flip overnight. Use the planning tools to define what changes, in what order, and what success looks like at each stage.

nodes.branch_name: Pivot — Unproven Direction: Test Before Committing
nodes.condition: New strategic direction is unproven — still at idea or intention stage with no market validation
nodes.action: Do not build a full strategic plan around an unproven pivot. Instead, use the Cautious Reveal Method to test market demand without exposing the business to unnecessary risk, or Lite Feasibility to assess whether the idea is commercially viable before committing. Cross-reference the profitability_feasibility advisory tree for the full Cautious Reveal and Trial Fit routing.
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Cross-reference the profitability_feasibility advisory tree (pf_cr_outreach for Cautious Reveal, pf_tf_qualify for Trial Fit, pf_feasibility_entry for Lite Feasibility). The Cautious Reveal Method is the safest way to test a new direction — it validates demand before the business invests significantly in the pivot.

nodes.branch_name: Growth Strategy — What Kind of Growth?
nodes.question: Is the business in a good position and planning its next growth phase proactively, is it stuck or stagnant and struggling to grow despite wanting to, or does the owner want to scale rapidly and is ready to move aggressively?

branches.answer_pattern: proactive / positioned well / business is performing / want to plan the next phase / deliberate growth
stuck / stagnant / frustrated / losing the battle / not growing despite trying
rapid scale / aggressive growth / wants to move fast / shoot for the stars / significant expansion

nodes.branch_name: Proactive Growth — Expanding Existing or Entering New?
nodes.question: Is the growth plan about expanding what already works — doing more of the same but at greater scale — or about entering new markets, launching new products, or pursuing a genuinely new opportunity?

branches.answer_pattern: expanding existing / more of what works / same product or service / grow the current model
new markets / new products / new opportunity / new customer segment / diversification

nodes.condition: Business wants to grow by expanding what already works — more volume, more reach, same core model
nodes.action: Use the Capacity, Capability, Opportunity framework to map the 'Probable Perfect World' output against 'Current World' performance and identify the capacity ceiling. Use a Revenue Model to run multiple what-if scenarios across volume, price, and cost levers. Use the Planning Outcomes Review to frame the growth targets formally. Cross-reference the capacity_capability_opportunity coaching tree for the full CCO facilitation sequence.
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Expanding the existing model is the lowest-risk growth strategy — it uses proven assets, known customers, and tested processes. The main constraint is almost always capacity (how much can the current model produce) or capability (does the team have the skills to sustain higher volume). The CCO framework surfaces both.

nodes.branch_name: Growth — New Markets or New Opportunities
nodes.condition: Business wants to grow by entering new markets, launching new products, or pursuing a new opportunity
nodes.action: Use the CCO Opportunity Validation process — demand proof of market demand before committing. Ask what qualitative data or customer feedback exists. Identify specific external drivers (event trigger, economy stimulus, technology wave, fashion driver, environmental impact). Use Porter's & Pine and Blue Ocean Strategy to find the competitive positioning for the new market. Validate financially with a Revenue Model before building a full plan.
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: New market and new product growth carries significantly higher risk than expanding an existing model. The CCO framework's core rule applies: 'Fall in love with your customers, not your product or service.' Validate external demand before internal enthusiasm drives the investment decision.

nodes.condition: Business is failing to grow despite the owner's efforts — frustrated, feeling like they are losing the battle
nodes.action: Cross-reference the frameworks_find advisory tree (ff_branch2_stagnant → Capacity, Capability, Opportunity). If the owner is disengaged or avoidant about the numbers, also cross-reference frameworks_find ff_branch5_financial (Working Capital Cycle). Use the Heald Matrix as a pattern interrupt if the owner is stuck in a mindset that is blocking forward movement — it helps them see that progress is within reach before diving into diagnostic tools.
nodes.notes: Cross-reference the frameworks_find advisory tree — it was designed precisely for this scenario. The CCO framework is the primary tool; the Heald Matrix is the pattern interrupt if the owner's mindset is the barrier before the strategic tools can land.

nodes.branch_name: Rapid Scale — Is the Business Ready?
nodes.question: Does the business have the systems, people, financial base, and capability to support rapid scaling right now — or is there a readiness gap that needs to be addressed first?
nodes.notes: The CCO 'NOT YET' concept is critical here. A client who wants to risk everything and shoot for the stars immediately needs to be gently slowed down — not to discourage the ambition, but to surface the evidence of why the business may not be ready and what specifically needs to change before the scale move is safe.

branches.answer_pattern: ready / has systems, people, and capital / processes are solid / team can handle it
not sure / needs assessment / feels ready but hasn't checked / hasn't thought it through

nodes.branch_name: Rapid Scale — Ready: Build the Scale Plan
nodes.condition: Business has the capability, systems, and capital base to support rapid scaling
nodes.action: Reveal the Growth Curve to show where the business sits across the 9 Growth Stages and what the next complexity layers look like at scale. Use the Planning Outcomes Review to build the scale plan formally. A Revenue Model and Forecast are essential — scaling without a financial model is flying blind. Consider also whether governance structures need to be upgraded before the scale move (cross-reference the governance advisory tree).
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Businesses that are genuinely ready to scale often fail not because of the market opportunity but because of internal complexity they did not anticipate. The Growth Curve and the planning tools together create a picture of what scale actually demands — not just what it promises.

nodes.branch_name: Rapid Scale — Not Yet Ready: Build the Readiness
nodes.condition: Owner is enthusiastic about rapid scaling but the business has a readiness gap in systems, people, or financial foundation
nodes.action: Introduce the CCO 'NOT YET' concept — this is not an admission of failure, it is a structured search for evidence. Ask: what specifically needs to change for the scale move to be safe? Use the CCO Capability Check to assess whether skill sets, tools, equipment, and financial resources are sufficient to 'play with the big boys'. Use Revenue Model scenarios to show what scale actually demands financially. Run a Forecast to map the funding requirement if a capital injection is needed.
templates: Revenue Model (What-if Analysis — choose by industry)
nodes.notes: Cross-reference the capacity_capability_opportunity coaching tree for the full CCO facilitation including the 'NOT YET' and over-optimism management sequences. The goal is not to kill the ambition — it is to build the specific readiness that makes the scale move safe and sustainable.

nodes.branch_name: Avoidant — Client Struggling to Engage With Strategy
nodes.condition: Client is too busy, overwhelmed, or avoidant when it comes to strategic thinking — cannot engage with planning conversations
nodes.action: Use the Heald Matrix as a pattern interrupt before any strategic tools are introduced. Ask 'Have you heard of the Heald Matrix?' — if no, write the title down and reveal the quadrants progressively (Turtle, Shotgun, Lab-Rat, Pioneer). Ask 'Where do you think you sit?' then 'WHAT makes you say that?' (never WHY). Once they identify their desired future state, ask 'IF you took one small baby step towards that, WHAT might that look like?' Close with 'Would you be open to exploring how we could work a little closer with you going forward?' and follow up within 14 days with a branded Heald Matrix. Cross-reference the heald_matrix coaching tree for the full facilitation sequence.
nodes.notes: Cross-reference the heald_matrix coaching tree for the full step-by-step facilitation. The Heald Matrix works because it creates self-relevance before any agenda is imposed. Do not introduce planning templates until the client has identified their own desired future state — let them pull, do not push.

trees.name: Due Diligence
trees.description: Guides advisors through business acquisition due diligence: Quality of Earnings analysis, legal and contract risk, revenue concentration, human capital dependency, tech stack compatibility, and working capital adequacy. Covers full DD investigations, quick red flag assessments, and seller preparation.

nodes.condition: Before asking anything — determine whether this is a buyer DD investigation, a quick red flag check, or seller preparation
nodes.notes: Due diligence is the structured process of verifying that what a seller claims about a business is accurate before a buyer commits to the purchase price. The standard of proof required is not 'does it look right?' — it is 'can every material claim be independently verified?'. Core concept: Quality of Earnings (QoE) — the process of stripping out non-recurring, owner-specific, or artificially inflated items from reported EBITDA to establish what the business truly earns on a sustainable, recurring basis. The Data Room is the document repository provided by the seller containing: financial statements (3+ years), tax returns, contracts, employment agreements, IP documentation, lease agreements, and HR records. Red Flag Report: a summary document identifying material risks that could affect the purchase price, deal structure, or decision to proceed. This is distinct from full DD — it is a pre-commitment screen to identify obvious problems before investing in the full process. Three entry paths exist: (1) Full DD — buyer or advisor conducting a comprehensive investigation of an acquisition target. (2) Red Flag / Sniff Test — quick pre-commitment check to identify deal-breakers before committing to full DD costs. (3) Seller Preparation — preparing the business to withstand incoming buyer due diligence.

branches.answer_pattern: buyer or advisor conducting full due diligence on an acquisition target
quick red flag check or sniff test — need to identify obvious problems before committing to full DD
seller preparing the business to withstand an incoming buyer due diligence process

nodes.condition: Starting full DD — first check is whether the reported profitability is real, recurring, and sustainable
nodes.question: Let's start with the financials. Does the business show high profitability, but also claim multiple one-time expenses, owner perks, or unusual add-backs? Or do the financials appear clean and consistent across multiple years?
nodes.notes: Quality of Earnings (QoE) Analysis: the process of normalising reported EBITDA by removing non-recurring items and owner-specific expenses to reveal the true sustainable earnings capacity. Common red flags that trigger a QoE: (1) Multiple 'one-time' expenses claimed across multiple years — by definition, a cost that recurs annually is not one-time; (2) Excessive owner perks run through the business — personal vehicles, travel, family wages, personal insurance; (3) Aggressive depreciation or amortisation schedules that reduce reported profit without reflecting true asset consumption; (4) Revenue timing manipulation — recognising revenue early or deferring costs to inflate a specific reporting period; (5) Related-party transactions at non-market rates — sales to associated entities at inflated prices or purchases from related parties at inflated costs. The QoE output produces Adjusted EBITDA — the earnings figure a rational buyer would use as the basis for valuation. Any add-back claimed by the seller must be individually verified and justified. Unsupported add-backs are a negotiation lever, not a fact. Cross-reference: valuation tree — the same Add Backs methodology used in the seller's Business Sale Assessment must be applied consistently here from the buyer's perspective. If the QoE reveals the Adjusted EBITDA is materially lower than the seller's claimed figure, initiate valuation renegotiation before proceeding.

branches.answer_pattern: multiple one-time expenses, owner perks, or aggressive add-backs identified — QoE analysis required to establish true Adjusted EBITDA
financials appear clean, consistent, and recurring — no significant add-back concerns identified

nodes.condition: Financial review complete — now check whether key contracts survive a change of ownership
nodes.question: Does the business rely heavily on a small number of major long-term supplier or customer contracts? And have those contracts been reviewed for Change of Control clauses?
nodes.notes: Change of Control Clauses: provisions in contracts that grant the counterparty the right to terminate, renegotiate, or require consent upon a change in the ownership of the contracting entity. In an acquisition, the moment the business changes hands, any contract containing a Change of Control clause may become void, terminable, or subject to renegotiation — including the most critical supplier and customer relationships. This is one of the most commonly overlooked deal risks and one of the most consequential. A buyer who discovers post-settlement that their three largest customer contracts contained Change of Control termination rights has effectively bought a business without its primary revenue. Mandate a legal review specifically searching for Change of Control clauses in: all major customer contracts (by revenue value); all key supplier agreements (especially exclusive or preferred supplier arrangements); franchise agreements; licensing agreements; lease agreements (landlord consent to assignment); banking covenants and loan agreements. Where Change of Control clauses are found: (1) Negotiate with the counterparty pre-settlement to obtain written consent to the transfer; (2) Restructure the deal to minimise the formal 'change of control' trigger (e.g., asset purchase rather than share purchase in some jurisdictions); (3) Price the risk into the purchase price — if consent cannot be obtained, the value of the affected contracts must be discounted or excluded. Cross-reference: valuation tree — intangible asset transferability node covers the same principle for IP and licences.

branches.answer_pattern: heavy reliance on a few long-term contracts identified — mandate legal review for Change of Control clauses before proceeding
no significant contract concentration or Change of Control risk — legal structure is clean

nodes.condition: Legal review complete — now check whether revenue is dangerously concentrated in a single client
nodes.question: Looking at the revenue breakdown — does any single client account for more than 20% of the business's total revenue? And what happens to that revenue if the relationship sours post-acquisition?
nodes.notes: Revenue concentration is one of the most significant valuation risk factors in any acquisition. The 20% threshold is the standard deal-risk trigger: a single client representing more than 20% of total revenue creates a fragile revenue base — the loss of that one relationship post-settlement could be catastrophic for the acquirer. Why concentration risk is amplified post-acquisition: (1) Key person risk — the concentrated client relationship is often personal to the selling owner. When the owner exits, the client's loyalty may exit with them; (2) Renegotiation leverage — a client who knows they represent 30% of your revenue knows they can demand better terms; (3) Credit risk amplification — if that single large client has payment problems, the cash flow impact is disproportionate. Action when >20% concentration is identified: (1) Flag as a high-risk deal-breaker — the deal should not proceed at the current asking price without addressing this risk; (2) Initiate valuation renegotiation — the revenue attributed to the concentrated client should be risk-adjusted (apply a higher discount rate or exclude projected future revenue from that client); (3) Investigate transferability — has the concentrated client been consulted? Do they have a Change of Control clause? Will they commit to the relationship post-sale in writing? (4) Consider a transitional earnout — structure a portion of the price as contingent on the concentrated client remaining for a defined period post-settlement. Healthy revenue distribution benchmark: no single client >15% of revenue; top 5 clients collectively <50% of revenue.

branches.answer_pattern: single client exceeds 20% of total revenue — flag as high-risk deal-breaker and initiate valuation renegotiation
revenue well distributed — no single client concentration risk above the 20% threshold

nodes.condition: Revenue concentration checked — now assess whether key person dependency threatens post-acquisition value
nodes.question: Are the business's operations, key client relationships, or primary sales accounts tied to a single key person — and is that person the seller who will be leaving at or shortly after settlement?
nodes.notes: Human capital risk in acquisitions mirrors the goodwill transferability problem in valuations — the buyer is paying for something that may walk out the door with the seller. Key person dependency assessment: (1) Map the organisational structure — who makes the critical decisions? Who owns the key client relationships? Who has the technical knowledge the business depends on? (2) Quantify the revenue at risk — what percentage of revenue could be attributed to the key person's direct relationships or capabilities? (3) Assess replaceability — is the key person's function documentable and trainable, or is it based on irreplaceable tacit knowledge or personal reputation? Earn-Out Clause: the primary deal mechanism for managing key person risk. Structured in the Sales and Purchase Agreement (SPA), an earn-out ties a defined portion of the total purchase price to the key person: (a) remaining with the business for a specified transition period post-settlement (typically 12-36 months); (b) achieving defined performance targets during that period. The earn-out converts the key person risk from a pre-settlement concern into a post-settlement performance incentive. Earn-out design considerations: the performance targets must be objectively measurable (revenue, gross profit, client retention rate); the earn-out period must be long enough to provide meaningful continuity but short enough to motivate the seller; the calculation methodology must be agreed in writing — disputes over earn-out calculations are a leading cause of post-acquisition litigation. Where the key person is NOT the seller — identify and assess dependency on any staff member. If critical staff are not contractually retained through the transition, negotiate retention bonuses or employment agreement amendments as a condition of settlement. Cross-reference: succession tree (management succession path) for the development framework to reduce key person dependency post-acquisition.

branches.answer_pattern: key person dependency identified — structure Earn-out clause in SPA tied to retention and performance targets
no significant key person dependency — management depth is adequate to survive the ownership transition

nodes.condition: Human capital risk assessed — now check whether the target's technology infrastructure is compatible with the buyer's
nodes.question: Does the buyer operate on modern cloud-based systems, while the target is running on legacy software or paper-based processes? And has anyone calculated what it will actually cost to bring the target's systems up to standard?
nodes.notes: Technology due diligence is consistently underestimated in acquisition assessments. Hidden integration costs — what the technology industry calls 'tech debt' — can be substantial and must be factored into the adjusted purchase price before settlement. Tech debt assessment: (1) Map the target's current technology stack — what software, hardware, and systems does the business currently rely on? Are they cloud-based or on-premise? Proprietary or standard? (2) Identify incompatibilities — where does the target's stack conflict with the buyer's existing systems? What data migration, integration, or replacement will be required? (3) Quantify the upgrade cost — obtain independent IT quotes for: software licensing and migration, hardware replacement, data migration and testing, staff retraining, and any parallel-running period where both systems must operate simultaneously. (4) IT & Cyber Audit: extend beyond compatibility to assess cybersecurity posture — has the target had any data breaches? What is their data governance standard? Are customer data handling practices compliant with applicable privacy legislation? Does the target have cyber insurance? A business with poor cybersecurity posture inherits that risk on day one of new ownership — including any pre-settlement breach that manifests post-settlement. Action: the total quantified tech debt figure is deducted from the purchase price in negotiation, or a specific budget allocation is negotiated as part of the settlement terms. Do not allow the buyer to discover tech debt post-settlement — it becomes a stranded cost with no recourse. Cross-reference: data_financial_systems tree for cloud platform selection and systems implementation advisory.

branches.answer_pattern: legacy or paper-based systems identified — trigger IT and Cyber Audit to quantify tech debt before finalising purchase price
systems compatible or target already on modern cloud platforms — no material tech debt identified

nodes.condition: Tech stack reviewed — now check whether the target's cash collection cycle creates a working capital funding gap for the buyer
nodes.question: Looking at the accounts receivable aging report — are clients consistently taking 90 or more days to pay? And has the total working capital requirement for the buyer been calculated based on actual trading cycles, not just the headline revenue figures?
nodes.notes: Working capital risk is the gap between when the business must pay its obligations and when it actually collects its revenue. In an acquisition, this gap becomes the buyer's problem from day one — and if it has not been quantified and funded in advance, the buyer can face a cash flow crisis in the first trading months despite the business being profitable on paper. Accounts Receivable Aging Analysis: review the full AR aging report — not just the total outstanding balance, but the distribution: current (0-30 days), 31-60 days, 61-90 days, 90+ days. A business with a high proportion of 90+ day debtors has a structural cash conversion problem that the buyer will inherit. Key calculation: Daily Cash Flow Requirement = (Monthly Fixed Costs + Monthly Variable Costs) ÷ 30. If clients consistently pay at 90 days, the buyer must be able to fund approximately 3 months of operating costs from their own capital or credit facilities before collections normalise. Additional working capital considerations: (1) Seasonal businesses — working capital requirements fluctuate significantly; the buyer must be funded for the worst-case seasonal trough, not the annual average; (2) Inventory and WIP — if the business carries significant stock or work in progress, these represent cash tied up that must be separately funded; (3) Supplier payment terms — does the business have favourable extended payment terms with key suppliers that the buyer may not inherit if the supplier reviews credit terms on ownership change? (4) Growth capital — if the acquisition plan includes growth, additional working capital beyond the base trading requirement must be planned and funded. The standard provision applied in the valuation tree (45-day trading reserve on top of the purchase price) is a minimum floor — if AR aging is 90+ days, the reserve must be recalculated accordingly. Cross-reference: forecasting_financial_mgmt tree (WC path) for working capital cycle diagnosis and cash tactics; cashflow tree for immediate cash flow management.

branches.answer_pattern: AR aging shows clients consistently taking 90+ days — recalculate daily cash flow requirements and adjust working capital provision accordingly
AR aging within normal trading terms — working capital position adequate at standard 45-day provision

nodes.branch_name: Final Report — DD Findings and Recommendation
nodes.condition: All six DD checks complete — consolidate findings and deliver a recommendation
nodes.notes: The Due Diligence Report consolidates the findings from all six investigation areas into a structured output that supports the buyer's final decision. Report structure: (1) Executive Summary — overall risk rating (Low / Medium / High / Do Not Proceed) and the primary basis for that rating; (2) Quality of Earnings Summary — Adjusted EBITDA vs reported EBITDA, list of verified and rejected add-backs, implied valuation impact; (3) Risk Register — for each of the six DD areas, document: the risk identified (if any), the severity (Low / Medium / High / Deal-Breaker), the recommended treatment, and the recommended price adjustment or deal structure change; (4) Recommended Deal Structure — based on the findings, what changes to the deal are recommended? This may include: price reduction to reflect identified risks; earn-out provisions for key person or performance risks; vendor finance component; specific warranties and indemnities in the SPA; conditions precedent (things the seller must resolve before settlement can occur); (5) Decision Recommendation — Proceed as structured / Proceed with renegotiation / Conditional proceed pending resolution of specific items / Do not proceed. Red Flag Report: if any single finding constitutes a deal-breaker (revenue concentration >20% with no mitigation, Change of Control clauses that cannot be resolved, QoE revealing Adjusted EBITDA materially below the valuation basis), the Red Flag Report documents this specifically and recommends suspension of the process pending resolution. The Red Flag Report is issued before full DD costs are incurred — it is the early-warning output. Templates: Due Diligence Checklist. Cross-reference: valuation tree for the 3-Point Sensitivities (serviceability, goodwill payback, performance variance) — all DD findings feed directly into the final valuation model.

nodes.branch_name: Red Flag / Sniff Test — Pre-Commitment Screen
nodes.condition: Advisor needs a quick pre-commitment screen to identify obvious deal-breakers before committing to full DD costs
nodes.notes: The Red Flag / Sniff Test is a rapid pre-DD screen — typically conducted before the buyer commits to the cost and time of a full due diligence investigation. It is not a substitute for full DD; it is a filter to avoid wasting resources on deals with obvious fatal flaws. Six quick checks that constitute the sniff test: (1) Does the seller's EBITDA rely heavily on add-backs? Ask for the breakdown of the Adjusted EBITDA — if more than 20-25% of the claimed profit relies on add-backs, demand immediate QoE before proceeding. (2) Does any single customer account for more than 20% of revenue? If yes, and if that customer has a personal relationship with the seller, this is a structural red flag. (3) Are the key contracts reviewed for Change of Control clauses? If not, mandate it before proceeding. (4) Is the business's goodwill primarily attached to the seller personally? If the seller's name is the brand, their relationships are the business, and they plan to exit immediately — the goodwill value is largely illusory. (5) Are the accounts receivable aging reports available and clean? Refusal or inability to provide AR aging is a behavioural red flag. Aged debtors may indicate customer disputes, quality issues, or revenue recognition problems. (6) Is there a documented management team, or is the seller the sole decision-maker? A business with no management depth cannot be acquired safely without a structured transition and earn-out. Sniff test outcome: if two or more of the six checks raise concerns, escalate to full DD immediately. If any single check reveals a deal-breaker-level issue, issue a formal Red Flag Report and suspend the process pending resolution. Do not allow deal enthusiasm to override early warning signals — the cost of proper DD is always less than the cost of a failed acquisition.

nodes.condition: Client is selling and needs to prepare the business to withstand a buyer's due diligence process
nodes.notes: Seller preparation for DD is the mirror image of the buyer's investigation — the goal is to ensure that every question a buyer will ask has a clean, documented, verifiable answer. A well-prepared seller reduces the buyer's perceived risk, which protects the asking price and reduces the likelihood of late-stage price chipping or deal withdrawal. Six preparation priorities matching the buyer's DD checklist: (1) Financial Preparation — engage an accountant to prepare a clean Quality of Earnings analysis from the seller's perspective before the buyer requests it. Identify all legitimate add-backs, document the basis for each, and prepare supporting evidence. Surprises in the financials discovered by the buyer's DD team are always more damaging than disclosures made proactively by the seller. (2) Contract Review — conduct a self-audit of all major contracts for Change of Control clauses before the buyer's legal team finds them. Where clauses exist, approach the counterparty proactively to negotiate consent — the seller's relationship with the counterparty is the best leverage they will ever have. (3) Revenue Concentration — if a single client represents >20% of revenue, begin proactively broadening the client base before going to market. This may require a 12-18 month preparation timeline. Alternatively, approach the concentrated client to obtain a long-term contract commitment that will survive the sale. (4) Key Person Dependency — systematically reduce personal goodwill: document processes, develop management depth, transition client relationships to the business rather than the individual. A business that runs well without the owner for 6 weeks is worth more than one that cannot. Cross-reference: exit preparation path in the valuation tree. (5) Technology — ensure all systems are cloud-based, current, and documented. A business on legacy systems will attract a tech debt discount in any negotiation. (6) Working Capital — ensure AR aging is clean before buyer review. Address aged debtors proactively — an AR aging report with significant 90+ day balances will trigger a working capital risk adjustment to the purchase price. Cross-reference: valuation tree (exit preparation path) for the full seller value-building framework.
